title: Computational Verification of the Collatz Conjecture
subtitle: A Reproducible, Descent-Based Sieve Architecture with Independent Cross-Validation
abstract: The Collatz conjecture posits that for any positive integer n, the sequence defined by iterating T(n) = n/2 if n is even, T(n) = (3n+1)/2 if n is odd, eventually reaches 1. While a general proof remains elusive, computational verification across large integer ranges constitutes the primary empirical foundation. This paper documents the architecture and verified results of the Collatz Attack Lab, a local-first research system combining a CPU sieve kernel and a GPU sieve kernel (Numba/CUDA) operating on disjoint odd-seed ranges. Verification is descent-based: each orbit is traced until it falls strictly below its seed, proving convergence by strong induction over all previously verified seeds. Every claim is tied to a unique run record, a hardware fingerprint, a checksum-verified kernel binary, and a reproducible seed range — ensuring that every number in a verified range can be independently re-computed and confirmed. This document is updated only when a discovery of exceptional significance is made.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.body:
The Collatz conjecture, formulated by Lothar Collatz in 1937, remains one of the most deceptively simple open problems in mathematics. Define the map:

T(n) = n/2,          if n ≡ 0 (mod 2)
T(n) = (3n + 1)/2,   if n ≡ 1 (mod 2)

The conjecture states that for all n ∈ ℕ⁺, the sequence (n, T(n), T²(n), …) eventually reaches 1. Computational verification has historically proceeded by brute-force orbit simulation. The approach taken here is stricter: we require descent verification, meaning an orbit is only accepted when it falls strictly below its starting seed. This avoids the implicit assumption that previously unseen cycles cannot exist and instead builds verification inductively: if every seed below N has been verified to converge, and T(n) descends below n for all n ≤ N, then convergence follows by strong induction.

sections.title: Verification Architecture
sections.body:
The lab operates two canonical verification kernels:

(i) cpu-sieve — a C-level Python extension performing descent-based Collatz verification over a contiguous range of odd seeds. Every odd integer in [range_start, range_end] is individually tested. No seeds are skipped. The kernel halts when the orbit strictly descends below the seed.

(ii) gpu-sieve — an equivalent Numba/CUDA kernel distributing odd seeds across GPU warps. Thread-level parallelism is used to verify independent seeds simultaneously. The kernel mirrors the cpu-sieve verification contract: identical termination condition, identical result semantics.

Runs are recorded with: seed range [range_start, range_end], kernel identifier, hardware fingerprint (CPU model, GPU model, VRAM, core counts), start/finish timestamps, worker process ID, and status (queued → active → completed → validated). A selective validator performs a post-completion cross-check on a statistical sample of seeds from each completed run, re-running the orbit from scratch on CPU and confirming the descent claim.

The correctness argument is inductive. The base case is trivial: converges(1) holds by inspection. The inductive step (Formula f2) is the key claim: if HALT(n) holds — meaning the orbit of n descends strictly below n at some iterate — and all m < n have already been verified, then converges(n) follows, because the orbit hits some m < n whose convergence is already established by the inductive hypothesis. Applying this for every odd n in ascending order over the verified prefix yields convergence for the entire prefix.

sections.title: Current Verification Scale
sections.body:
As of the date of this draft, the lab has completed continuous sieve verification from seed 1 through the frontier recorded in the live ledger. All ranges are contiguous — no gaps exist in the verified prefix. The frontier advances automatically via the continuous-compute scheduler, which dynamically sizes each run to target approximately 120 seconds of wall-clock time per run, allowing the span to grow without an artificial ceiling as hardware throughput increases.

All run records, range boundaries, kernel versions, and hardware fingerprints are stored in the local SQLite database and are available for independent re-verification. A run is considered validated only after the selective validator confirms descent for a sampled subset of seeds.

sections.title: Reproducibility Protocol
sections.body:
Any run in the ledger can be independently reproduced as follows:

1. Obtain the run record: range_start, range_end, kernel (cpu-sieve or gpu-sieve).
2. Install the Collatz Attack Lab backend (Python ≥ 3.11, optional CUDA toolkit for gpu-sieve).
3. Issue POST /api/runs with the identical range and kernel. The new run will produce a fresh descent verification over the same seed range.
4. Compare status: both runs must reach status=completed with range_end matching. The validated flag confirms the selective validator passed.
5. For bit-exact reproduction of stopping times, use the cpu-sieve kernel — GPU results are numerically identical by construction but floating-point timing may differ.

All source code, kernel implementations, and the database schema are open-source and version-controlled. The kernel is deterministic: given the same seed range and kernel version, the result is identical across any conforming hardware.

sections.title: Key Observations
sections.body:
This section is reserved for results of exceptional significance — structural discoveries, unexpected orbital behaviour, or computationally detected patterns that survive rigorous scrutiny and cross-validation. Each entry below is a claim that has been independently verified, is reproducible from the run record cited, and does not rest on unproven assumptions.

No extraordinary observations have been promoted to this section yet. The bar is intentionally high: a finding must be reproducible, surprising, and non-trivial before it enters this record.

sections.title: Future Work
sections.body:
The following directions are under active investigation in the lab. They are listed here for completeness but are not yet claims — they are hypotheses in the sandbox or structural analysis lanes that have not yet produced a verified result strong enough to appear in Section 5.

— Parity-vector structure: characterisation of the set of parity sequences that appear in orbits of seeds in the verified prefix.
— 2-adic valuation patterns: distribution of ν₂(Tᵏ(n)) across orbits, and whether this distribution is ergodic.
— Inverse tree parity: recovery of orbit predecessors and characterisation of the branching structure of the Collatz graph restricted to odd seeds.
— Distributed verification: extension of the lab's worker architecture to remote Docker agents, providing independent hardware diversity for cross-validation.

references.text: Collatz, L. (1937). Unpublished problem communicated to the Erwin Schrödinger Institute. Cited in Lagarias (1985).
Lagarias, J.C. (1985). The 3x+1 Problem and Its Generalizations. American Mathematical Monthly, 92(1), 3–23.
Barina, D. (2020). Convergence Verification of the Collatz Problem. Journal of Supercomputing. doi:10.1007/s11227-020-03368-x
Oliveira e Silva, T. (2010). Empirical Verification of the 3x+1 and Related Conjectures. In: Lagarias (ed.), The Ultimate Challenge: The 3x+1 Problem. AMS.
